course_description:
This course introduces students to the characteristics, relationships, dynamics and complexity of the international tourism, hospitality and event industries. The course covers key topics such as definitions and concepts of tourism, hospitality and events; the nature and size of the tourism, hospitality and event industries; economic characteristics and employment outcomes; demand and supply aspects including the role of intermediaries, tourism operators, accommodation, food and beverage and events; and current trends and impacts. This course also enhances student employability by building critical thinking and academic writing skills.

This foundation course provides commencing postgraduate coursework students with the requisite knowledge of the tourism, hospitality and events industries to enable further postgraduate coursework studies within this area. Students are challenged throughout the course to understand the complexity of the tourism, hospitality and events industries and the economic, social, cultural and environmental implications of tourist activity using international and Australian examples.

Sustainable Development Goals - UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of the United Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME) . As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through the Sustainable Development Goals . The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.

course_aims: Given its nature as a foundation course, TOUR7020 aims for students to develop a comprehensive knowledge of the key theories and concepts in tourism, hospitality and events. This knowledge will form a basis for future tourism, hospitality and event postgraduate coursework studies.ﾠEmployability is embedded in this course allowing students to build their critical thinking and academic writing skills.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and analyse the impacts tourism, hospitality and events have on sustainability.
Apply systems thinking to analyse the sustainability of a tourism, hospitality, event system.
Develop and use critical thinking and academic writing skills to complete all assessment tasks.
Collaboratively work in teams to prepare a report on a tourism, hospitality, event system.

assessment_details.title: Impact Identification
assessment_details.task_description: Note : Assessment 1 (Impact Identification), Assessment 2 (Impact Analysis) and Assessment 3 (System Report) are related. The planning and feedback from Assessment 1 will be used by you to develop Assessment 2. Likewise, the planning and feedback from Assessment 2 will be used by you to develop Assessment 3. This is an individual task. 2000 words (excluding template headings) As an expert in sustainability, you have been engaged by the management team of a tourism/ hospitality/ event (T/H/E) activity to evaluate the sustainability of the activity. Your task: Identify a T/H/E activity that you will evaluate. This can be an accommodation establishment; an event; or a tourist attraction. It can be an activity you can visit or one that you know well. Each activity can only be done by one student. Do NOT choose South Bank (Brisbane), Minjerribah (North Stradbroke Island) or Lady Elliot Island Eco Resort. By Week 4, you MUST get your T/H/E activity approved by the teaching team. Identify the positive and negative impacts that your activity has on the environment, local community/ culture, and the local economy. Determine if the impacts you identified at your T/H/E activity are consistent with impacts identified in the literature. Note : Your research will ONLY be based on an analysis of available literature in the field. This mean you will NOT collect primary data (i.e., you will NOT do any 'empirical' research). This means your team will NOT make any contact with your T/H/E business. The impacts are from YOUR observations only. The structure of your evaluation will be explained in more detail during your course seminars. Use of AI/MT Technologies Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that can support this type of activity. Therefore, you are allowed to appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. However, in every instance where AI or MT is used, you must clearly reference its use. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Format : Font size 11 point Font type - Calibri; Times New Roman; Arial Line spacing - 1.5 spacing Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Your completed Impact Identification must be submitted to Turnitin, which is accessed from Blackboard through the Assessment link.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Impact Analysis
assessment_details.task_description: This is an individual task 2000 words Your Task: Analyse the impacts you identified in your Impact Identification (Assessment 1) to determine the status of your T/H/E activity in relation to sustainability. Then provide 3-6 recommendations that the manager of your T/H/E activity could implement to maintain, or improve, sustainability of the activity. Your Impact Analysis must include the following sections: Introduction (approximately 150-200 words) Brief overview of your T/H/E activity (type of activity, size, location; privately owned or government owned etc) This can be an improved version of the introduction for your Impact Identification . Sustainability Status (approximately 300 words) Analyse the impacts you identified to draw your own conclusion about the status of sustainability at your T/H/E activity. Remember, you are not restating the impacts: you are considering them collectively to make a reasoned assessment. Recommendations (approximately 1500 words) Each recommendation must be one clear action. 'Educate visitors', for example, is too vague. Instead, ask yourself questions to help shape your recommendation, in this example: what do you need to educate visitors about? Why? How should this be done? What will the impact be on sustainability? The recommendation might then read more like this: Educate visitors about the important role the native bees play in pollinating the plant life. Importantly this will include teaching visitors how to interact safely with the bees. This can be done by..... For each recommendation, provide evidence that is reliable, such as from academic journals, government websites, etc. Consider for example: Has something similar been implemented successfully somewhere else? Why do you believe it would be appropriate in your context? How should it be implemented? Each recommendation must be at least 200 words . You could have 3 recommendations of 500 words each, or 7 recommendations of 200 words each, or a combination of some longer and some shorter recommendations. Your recommendations should cover the whole triple bottom line (TBL). Note : Your research will ONLY be based on an analysis of available literature in the field. This mean you will NOT collect primary data (i.e., you will NOT do any 'empirical' research). This means your team will NOT make any contact with your T/H/E business. Use of AI/MT Technologies Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that can support this type of activity. Therefore, you are allowed to appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. However, in every instance where AI or MT is used, you must clearly reference its use. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Referencing: APA7 Formatting: Font size - 11 point Font type - Calibri; Times New Roman; Arial Line spacing - 1.5 spacing Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: System Report
assessment_details.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution
assessment_details.due_date: Formative Buddycheck - 2:00pm 7/10/2025 Oral Q&A Check-In During Class Week 11 - Week 13 System Report - 2:00pm 13/11/2025 Buddycheck - 5:00pm 13/11/2025
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessed. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This is a team task (3-4 students per team) 4000 words (excluding title page; executive summary; table of contents; reference list; appendices – if used). Your Task: Due to your expertise in tourism, hospitality and event (THE) management, your team has been hired as consultants to prepare a System Report for the manager of a T/H/E business. The T/H/E business you will be preparing and writing the report for is the tourism OR hospitality OR event T/H/E business you have already been working on in class. Your team members will be the group of students you were assigned to work with in class. AS A TEAM: Prepare a report for the manager of your T/H/E business. Your report will be based on a tourism system developed for one (1) potential, or existing, target market for your T/H/E business. Include the following in your report: Title page Executive summary (overview of your whole report) Table of contents (use numbered headings) Introduction (explain why systems thinking is beneficial for your T/H/E business) 'Body' of your report (Do not use the heading 'body'. Instead, use headings appropriate for your topic). In this section, include: Diagram of your system Explanation of your system Relevance of sustainability for your system Conclusion (summary of the key take-away points for your T/H/E business manager) Recommendations (analyse the recommendations from each team member's Impact Analysis (Assessment 2) to determine, and justify, three (3) strategies that your T/H/E business manager could implement to support/ encourage a sustainable system. Each recommendation should be approximately 200 words. Reference list (at least 10 academic references published after 2014 - these can include the sources used for your Impact Identification and Impact Analysis ) Appendices (if needed) Note : Your research will ONLY be based on an analysis of available literature in the field. This mean you will NOT collect primary data (i.e., you will NOT do any 'empirical' research). This means your team will NOT make any contact with your T/H/E business. Referencing : APA7 Formatting : Font size - 11 point Font type - Calibri; Times New Roman; Arial Line spacing - 1.5 spacing Marking Criteria: To ensure all team members are contributing equally to the report, some compulsory check points have been put in place for this assessment task. These check points are designed to alert you/ your teacher to any issues your and/or your team are experiencing, and allow you time to rectify these issues prior to submission. Checkpoints: Week 10 Buddycheck At the end of the Week 10 seminar you will INDIVIDUALLY complete a formative online Buddycheck that will allow you to evaluate and comment on team members' contributions to the team project. Weeks 11/ 12 Oral Check-in During one of these seminars, your teacher will take you aside and ask you three (3) randomly allocated questions designed to verify your contribution to the team report AND your understanding of course content to date. These will be broad questions linked to concepts related to your team report, i.e., not just your section/s of the report. You will not be asked about specific theories. NOTE: If you answer the questions satisfactorily during this check-in, you will have no further oral check-ins. Your teacher may ask you an additional question/s if they feel this will help you meet the requirements of this check-in. If you are unable to answer the questions satisfactorily, you will be required to complete the Week 13 check-in. Week 13 Oral Check-in This check-in is only for students who require another opportunity to demonstrate their satisfactory progress/understanding. The format will be the same as that used in the Week 11/12 oral check-in, i.e., your teacher will take you aside and ask you three (3) randomly allocated questions designed to verify your contribution to the team report AND your understanding of course content to date. NOTE: Your teacher may ask you an additional question/s if they feel this will help you meet the requirements of this check-in. If you are unable to answer the questions satisfactorily, you will receive a mark of 0/40 for Assessment 3. Please Note: The Q&A sessions will be recorded for marking purposes as per UQ Policy. Exam Block Buddycheck As you will have learnt from the Week 10 Buddycheck (peer evaluation), each team member will be allocated a score by Buddycheck. This score will determine the final mark you receive for Assessment 3. For example: If your team mark for the System Report is 30/40 and your Buddycheck score is .8, you will get 24/40 (30 x .8) for your System Report mark. If your Buddycheck score is 1.0 or above you will receive the full marks allocated for your team's report (30/40). NOTE: This means it is possible for each team member to receive a different mark for this assessment task, and highlights the importance of contributing equally to this team-based assessment task. Use of AI/MT Technologies Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that can support this type of activity. Therefore, you are allowed to appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. However, in every instance where AI or MT is used, you must clearly reference its use. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, you will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of your written submission independent of AI and MT tools . A fuller explanation (and examples) of what is required will be discussed in class. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to tourism, hospitality and events (THE) What is THE? Exploring your Course Profile.
Sustainability Sustainability - a triple bottom line approach. Assessment 1 explained. Appropriate use of AI in TOUR7020.
Advanced database searching and Referencing Advanced Database Searching workshop - location to be advised. APA7 Referencing explained.
Sustainability: economic impacts Using the tourism multiplier to explore economic impacts on the local economy. Student Central - how can we help you?

Sustainability: socio-cultural impacts Exploring the impacts THE have on host societies and culture. We consider what authenticity means for THE and whether it is compromised through cultural commodification. Critical thinking and analysis - expectations for TOUR7020.

Sustainability: environmental impacts This week we'll meet at South Bank Brisbane to evaluate the impact of THE on sustainability. This excursion will help you understand the expectations for Assessment 1. Before you attend the excursion be sure to complete the interactive workbook on the impacts of THE on the natural and built environments. You'll find the workbook on Blackboard. Details about the excursion will be provided in class.

Sustainability - managing impacts We'll learn how Lady Elliot Island Eco Resort became an award winning sustainable resort. We'll experience the benefits of stakeholder collaboration by playing the Sustainable Island Game.
Systems theory and THE Systems and sustainability - the link. Progressing your analytical skills - using Assessment 1 to prepare Assessment 2.
Tourism System - tourist and generating region Exploring what we need to know about a tourist's home town. Assessment 3 explained. Strategies for working in groups.
In-Semester Break No classes this week.

Tourism System - transit region Exploring transit regions and how we can influence the way tourists interact with them. Group work on Assessment 3 - what is your transit region and how can it be used? Compulsory Check Point - Buddy Check Work Integrated Learning (WIL) opportunities at UQ.

Tourism System - destination region (tourist perspective) Exploring the variety of destinations and what tourists expect a destination to provide. Group work on Assessment 3 - what is your destination and what expectations does your target market have of your destination? Compulsory Oral Check Point for Assessment 3.

Tourism System - destination region (management perspective) Strategies to manage destinations to meet tourists' expectations. Group work on Assessment 3 - which management strategies are relevant for your destination? Writing an executive summary. Compulsory Oral Check Point for Assessment 3.

Tourism System - external influences Exploring the external influences that can impact a tourism system. Group work on Assessment 3 - what external influences are relevant for your system? Last chance - Compulsory Oral Check Point for Assessment 3.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
